title: Our Environment
summary: Chapter 13 covers the ecosystem, food chains, food webs, and environmental issues. Students learn about biotic and abiotic components, decomposers, trophic levels, and energy flow through ecosystems. Biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances are distinguished. Environmental problems such as ozone layer depletion (CFC), biological magnification of chemicals through food chain, and waste management are discussed.
learningPoints: Define ecosystem and its components: biotic and abiotic
Explain food chain and food web with examples
Define trophic levels: producers (1st), primary consumers (2nd), secondary consumers (3rd), tertiary consumers (4th)
State that only 10% of energy transfers to next trophic level (10% law)
Define biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances with examples
Explain biological magnification: accumulation of chemicals increases at higher trophic levels
State that highest trophic level organisms are most affected by biological magnification
Explain ozone layer and its importance: blocks UV rays
State that CFC (chlorofluorocarbons) cause ozone depletion
Explain the 3R principle: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle

laws.name: 10% Energy Flow Law
laws.statement: Only 10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next trophic level. 90% is lost as heat, used in metabolism, etc. This limits food chains to 3-4 levels.

laws.name: Ozone Protection
laws.statement: Ozone (O₃) layer in stratosphere absorbs harmful UV radiation from sun. CFC gases destroy ozone molecules. UV radiation causes skin cancer, cataracts, and crop damage.

formulas.label: Trophic Levels
formulas.expression: Producers → Primary consumers → Secondary consumers → Tertiary consumers
formulas.description: Example: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Hawk. Each level transfers 10% of energy to next.

formulas.label: Ozone Formation
formulas.description: Ozone forms in stratosphere by UV action on oxygen. CFC destroys this balance.

diagrams.title: Food Chain and Web
diagrams.description: Food chain: linear sequence (Grass → Rabbit → Fox → Lion). Food web: multiple interconnected food chains. More interconnections = more stable ecosystem.

diagrams.title: Trophic Levels Energy Pyramid
diagrams.description: Energy pyramid: producers at base (widest), each level above is narrower (10% rule). Biomass and energy decrease at each higher trophic level.

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka SSLC Science textbook Chapter 13 Our Environment.

resources.label: NCERT Science Textbook Class 10
resources.description: NCERT Class 10 Science Chapter 15 Our Environment.

resources.label: Our Environment — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on ecosystems, food chains, and environmental issues.

pyqs.question: Ozone layer is a boon to life on Earth. Justify. Which trophic level is most affected by biological magnification?
pyqs.answer: Ozone layer: blocks harmful UV radiation from sun protecting life. Without it: skin cancer, cataracts, crop failure. Biological magnification: highest trophic level organisms most affected. Harmful chemicals (DDT, pesticides) accumulate and concentrate at each level. 4th level accumulates most.

pyqs.question: What are biodegradable substances? Which synthetic chemical causes ozone depletion? Write two examples of decomposers.
pyqs.answer: Biodegradable: substances that decompose by microorganism activity (e.g. food, paper, cotton). CFC (chlorofluorocarbons) cause ozone depletion. Decomposers: bacteria and fungi.

pyqs.question: State events that occur during photosynthesis.
pyqs.answer: (i) Absorption of light energy by chlorophyll (ii) Conversion of light energy to chemical energy (iii) Splitting of water molecules (H₂O → H⁺ + O₂) (iv) Reduction of CO₂ to carbohydrates (glucose).

mcqs.question: CFC gases are responsible for:
mcqs.explanation: CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) from refrigerants and aerosols rise to stratosphere and destroy ozone molecules → UV radiation reaches Earth.

mcqs.question: Ozone layer is important because:
options: B. It absorbs harmful UV radiation
mcqs.explanation: Ozone (O₃) in stratosphere absorbs harmful UV radiation. Without it: skin cancer, cataracts, immune damage, crop failure.

mcqs.question: Organisms most affected by biological magnification are:
options: D. Organisms at highest trophic level
mcqs.explanation: Biological magnification: harmful chemicals (DDT) accumulate and concentrate at each trophic level. Highest level organisms accumulate most.

mcqs.question: Examples of decomposers are:
mcqs.explanation: Decomposers break down dead organic matter — bacteria and fungi. They recycle nutrients back to soil.

mcqs.question: Biodegradable substances are:
mcqs.explanation: Biodegradable: decomposed by microorganisms. Examples: food, paper, cotton, wood. Non-biodegradable: DDT, plastics, glass, metals.

mcqs.question: The 10% energy law means:
options: B. 10% of energy transfers to next trophic level
C. 90% of energy stored as food
mcqs.explanation: Only 10% of energy at one trophic level is available to the next. 90% lost as heat and in metabolic processes. This limits food chains to 3-4 levels.

mcqs.question: A food chain has 4 trophic levels. If producers have 10000J, energy at 4th level is:
mcqs.explanation: 10000 → 1000 (10%) → 100 (10%) → 10 (10%). Fourth trophic level has 10J by the 10% law.

mcqs.question: Synthetic chemical responsible for ozone depletion:
mcqs.explanation: CFC (chlorofluorocarbons) from refrigerants, air conditioners, spray cans rise to stratosphere and destroy ozone by releasing chlorine radicals.

mcqs.question: Which is NOT a component of an ecosystem?
mcqs.explanation: All three — producers, consumers, and decomposers — are biotic components of an ecosystem. Plus abiotic factors (temperature, water, light, soil).

mcqs.question: The 3R principle for environment protection stands for:
mcqs.explanation: 3Rs: Reduce (use less), Reuse (use again), Recycle (convert waste to new material). This minimises waste and environmental damage.

mcqs.question: Which trophic level has maximum energy?
mcqs.explanation: Producers (plants) fix solar energy through photosynthesis — they have maximum energy. Each level above has 10% of the level below.

mcqs.question: UV radiation can cause:
mcqs.explanation: UV radiation (not blocked by damaged ozone): causes skin cancer (melanoma), cataracts, weakened immune system, reduced crop yields.

mcqs.question: Food web is different from food chain because:
options: A. Food web has one linear path
B. Food web has multiple interconnected chains
C. Food web has only 2 trophic levels
D. Food chain has more stability
mcqs.explanation: Food web = multiple interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. More interconnections = greater stability (if one organism disappears, others can adapt).

mcqs.question: DDT accumulated in water is most concentrated in:
mcqs.explanation: DDT concentrates at each trophic level through biological magnification. Top predators (eagles, large fish) have the highest DDT concentration.

mcqs.question: The function of decomposers in an ecosystem is:
options: C. Break down dead matter and recycle nutrients
mcqs.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria, fungi) break down dead plants and animals → release nutrients back to soil → used by producers → completes nutrient cycle.

exercises.question: What is biological magnification? Which trophic level is most affected?
exercises.answer: Biological magnification: accumulation and increasing concentration of harmful chemicals (DDT, pesticides) at each successive trophic level in a food chain. Water (0.003 ppm) → phytoplankton (0.04 ppm) → zooplankton (0.5 ppm) → fish (2 ppm) → birds (25 ppm). Highest trophic level organisms are most affected.

exercises.question: Explain why ozone layer is called a boon for life on Earth.
exercises.answer: Ozone (O₃) in stratosphere absorbs 97-99% of harmful UV radiation from sun. Without ozone: (i) UV causes skin cancer and melanoma (ii) Cataracts and blindness (iii) Weakened immune system (iv) Reduced crop yields and phytoplankton damage. CFC gases destroy ozone — international ban under Montreal Protocol.

exercises.question: Distinguish between biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances with examples.
exercises.answer: Biodegradable: decomposed by microorganisms (bacteria, fungi) in nature. Examples: food waste, paper, leather, cotton, wood, agricultural waste. Non-biodegradable: NOT broken down by microorganisms, persist in environment. Examples: DDT, plastics, glass, metals, synthetic fibres. Non-biodegradable substances cause long-term environmental pollution.

exercises.question: Explain the 10% energy flow law with an example food chain.
exercises.answer: Only 10% of energy at one trophic level passes to the next — 90% is lost as heat, used in respiration, excretion. Example: Grass (100,000J) → Grasshopper (10,000J) → Frog (1,000J) → Snake (100J) → Eagle (10J). This limits food chains to 3-4 levels as very little energy remains.

exercises.question: What is the 3R principle? How does it help the environment?
exercises.answer: 3Rs: Reduce (use fewer resources, avoid waste), Reuse (use items multiple times instead of discarding), Recycle (convert waste into new usable material — paper, glass, metal). Benefits: reduces landfill waste, saves natural resources, reduces pollution, saves energy, protects ecosystem.

exercises.question: Construct a food chain with 4 trophic levels. If producers have 10000J, calculate energy at each level.
exercises.answer: Food chain: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Hawk. Energy: Grass = 10000J. Grasshopper = 10% = 1000J. Frog = 10% = 100J. Hawk = 10% = 10J. Only 0.1% of original energy reaches the top predator.

exercises.question: What are decomposers? Why are they essential in an ecosystem?
exercises.answer: Decomposers: organisms (bacteria, fungi) that break down dead and decaying organic matter into simple inorganic substances. Essential because: (i) Recycle nutrients back to soil for producers to use (ii) Prevent accumulation of dead matter (iii) Maintain nutrient cycle (carbon, nitrogen cycle) (iv) Essential for ecosystem functioning.

exercises.question: How are food chains and food webs related? Why is a food web more stable than a food chain?
exercises.answer: Food chain: linear sequence of organisms where each eats the next. Food web: network of many interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Food web is more stable because if one species disappears, other food pathways exist. In a single food chain, loss of one species could collapse the entire chain.
